name: Prabhakara Mishra
dates_notes: Mīmāṃsaka, Kumārila's near-contemporary; dates fixed by Kataoka and Frauwallner to the late seventh and early eighth century. The traditional account that Prabhākara was Kumārila's student is doubted by modern scholarship; what is secure is that the two schools — Bhāṭṭa and Prābhākara — develop in close dialectical dependence and disagree on systemic questions of cognition, sentence-meaning, and the analysis of error.

lineage_polemical.note: Prabhākara's Bṛhatī develops the Prābhākara position in sustained dialectical opposition to Kumārila's Bhāṭṭa school on triputī-saṃvit, anvitābhidhāna, and the analysis of error.

core_thesis: Prabhākara Miśra's *Bṛhatī* founds the Prābhākara school of Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā, which differs from Kumārila's Bhāṭṭa school on three systemic issues. First, *triputī-saṃvit*: every cognition is jointly self-revealing (*sva-prakāśa*), object-revealing (*viṣaya-prakāśa*), and subject-revealing (*āśraya-prakāśa*), in a single act (*Bṛhatī* on Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra 1.1.5, in the *Tarka-Pāda*; developed further in Śālikanātha's *Prakaraṇa-Pañcikā*, *Pramāṇa-Pārāyaṇa*). The Bhāṭṭa view that cognition reveals its object directly but is itself cognised only by inference (*jñātatā*) is rejected as needlessly complex. Second, *anvitābhidhāna*: the words of a sentence directly denote not their isolated meanings but their meanings-as-mutually-related; sentence-meaning is not constructed downstream from word-meanings but is the primary semantic content (*Bṛhatī* on MS 1.1.24–25, *vākyādhikaraṇa*). The Bhāṭṭa *abhihitānvaya* — words first denote isolated meanings, then those meanings combine — is rejected as both phenomenologically false and explanatorily inert. Third, *akhyāti*: erroneous cognition (the rope-as-snake) is analysed as the concurrent presence of two distinct cognitions, one perceptual and one memorial, whose distinctness is overlooked (*Bṛhatī* on MS 1.1.4, *pratyakṣa-sūtra*; developed in Śālikanātha's *Prakaraṇa-Pañcikā*, *Nayaviveka* section). There is no positive misrepresentation; there is only a non-discrimination of two real cognitive contents. The position is the cleanest realist analysis of error in classical Indian epistemology. The school's enduring philosophical influence is on the analysis of *vidhi* (injunctive force) — Prabhākara holds that *codanā* operates by directly producing a *kārya* (a duty-content) in the agent, whence the school's name *kāryānvitābhidhāna-vāda* (*Bṛhatī* on MS 1.1.2, *codanā-sūtra*) — and on the doctrine of cognition's intrinsic self-revelation, which Advaita partly inherits and modifies in service of *sākṣi-caitanya*.

engaged_works.title: Brhati
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Securely authored sub-commentary on Śabara's Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra-Bhāṣya; the founding text of the Prābhākara school.
engaged_works.summary: Sub-commentary on Śabara's *Mīmāṃsā-Sūtra-Bhāṣya*, working through the *Sūtra* and *Bhāṣya* in the order in which they treat the analysis of *dharma*, *codanā*, *vidhi*, *arthavāda*, and the principles of ritual hermeneutics (covering MS 1.1–1.4 fully; with later *tantras* covering further *adhyāyas* in fragmentary form). The *Bṛhatī*'s philosophical innovations are concentrated in the early sections on *codanā* (MS 1.1.2) and *śabda-pramāṇa* (MS 1.1.5). The *triputī-saṃvit* doctrine is developed in the analysis of *jñāna*-self-luminosity (on MS 1.1.4–5). The *anvitābhidhāna* doctrine is developed in the analysis of how injunctive sentences produce *kārya*-cognition (on MS 1.1.24–25): the analysis turns on the observation that the child learning language acquires the meaning of *gām ānaya* ("bring the cow") not by first acquiring the isolated meanings of *gām* and *ānaya* and then combining them, but by hearing the sentence used in context and inferring the relational meaning directly. The *akhyāti* analysis of error is developed in the discussion of how injunctive cognition can be effective even when its phenomenological content is partly mistaken (on MS 1.1.4, *pratyakṣa-sūtra*). Śālikanātha Miśra's *Ṛju-Vimalā* and *Prakaraṇa-Pañcikā* are the two standard sub-commentaries through which the *Bṛhatī*'s positions reach later medieval Indian philosophy. Vāchaspati Miśra and Vijñānabhikṣu both engage Prābhākara positions critically.
